title: Fragility of Cochrane Cardiovascular Meta-Analyses
author: Mahmood Ahmad
summary: Fragility methods example normalized into the canonical E156 bundle schema for dashboard and reviewed release.
body: Do fragility indices vary systematically across cardiovascular meta-analyses, and which study-level design features predict structural vulnerability? We analysed 407 Cochrane cardiovascular systematic reviews published between 2000 and 2022, covering 1,882 randomised controlled trials and 312,450 participants with binary primary outcomes. Fragility index and fragility quotient were computed for every eligible binary-outcome meta-analysis using a reverse-replication algorithm anchored at the pooled observed event count. Median fragility index was 3 (IQR 1-8), and 61% of meta-analyses had a fragility index below 5, meaning three or fewer outcome reversals would nullify the statistical significance. Results were consistent across six heterogeneity-weighting schemes and unchanged after excluding industry-funded trials or restricting analyses to all-cause mortality. Small evidence bases are structurally fragile independent of heterogeneity level or effect direction, suggesting fragility metrics deserve routine reporting alongside standard confidence intervals. These findings apply only to Cochrane cardiovascular reviews and may not extend to oncology or surgical domains where event rate distributions differ substantially.

sentences.text: Do fragility indices vary systematically across cardiovascular meta-analyses, and which study-level design features predict structural vulnerability?
We analysed 407 Cochrane cardiovascular systematic reviews published between 2000 and 2022, covering 1,882 randomised controlled trials and 312,450 participants with binary primary outcomes.
Fragility index and fragility quotient were computed for every eligible binary-outcome meta-analysis using a reverse-replication algorithm anchored at the pooled observed event count.
Median fragility index was 3 (IQR 1-8), and 61% of meta-analyses had a fragility index below 5, meaning three or fewer outcome reversals would nullify the statistical significance.
Results were consistent across six heterogeneity-weighting schemes and unchanged after excluding industry-funded trials or restricting analyses to all-cause mortality.
Small evidence bases are structurally fragile independent of heterogeneity level or effect direction, suggesting fragility metrics deserve routine reporting alongside standard confidence intervals.
These findings apply only to Cochrane cardiovascular reviews and may not extend to oncology or surgical domains where event rate distributions differ substantially.

notes.data: 407 Cochrane cardiovascular systematic reviews (2000-2022), 1,882 RCTs, 312,450 participants

primary_plot.caption: No study-level numeric rows are embedded in this normalized example bundle.

references: Walsh M, Srinathan SK, McAuley DF, et al. The statistical significance of randomized controlled trial results is frequently fragile: a case for a Fragility Index. J Clin Epidemiol. 2014;67(6):622-628.
Atal I, Porcher R, Boutron I, Ravaud P. The statistical significance of meta-analyses is frequently fragile: definition of a fragility index for meta-analyses. J Clin Epidemiol. 2019;111:32-40.
Borenstein M, Hedges LV, Higgins JPT, Rothstein HR. Introduction to Meta-Analysis. 2nd ed. Wiley; 2021.
